title: Dependency Parsing
concept: Dependency parsing maps direct binary links (dependencies) from head words to dependent words, showing grammatical connections (e.g. subject -> verb).

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of dependency parsing in NLP?

options.text: Finding direct links between related words
Counting the number of letters in a sentence
Translating words into a foreign language

exercises.explain: Dependency parsing focuses on finding direct grammatical connections (dependencies) between words, such as linking a subject to its verb.

cards.title: Who is Connected to Whom?
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how computers actually understand who did what in a sentence? Instead of just looking at lists of words, AI uses dependency parsing to draw invisible connecting lines, mapping out exactly how words interact!

cards.title: What is Dependency Parsing?
cards.body: Dependency parsing is a way of analyzing a sentence by finding direct grammatical links between individual words. Every link connects a boss word (the 'head') to a helper word (the 'dependent'). For example, in 'loud music', 'music' is the head, and 'loud' is the dependent.

cards.title: The Office Chart
cards.body: Think of dependency parsing like an organizational chart of a company. Instead of grouping people into general departments (like constituents), it draws direct lines from a manager (the head word) to their direct reports (the dependent words). If the manager leaves, the report has no anchor.

cards.title: Dependency Parsing in Action
cards.body: In the sentence 'Cats drink milk', the main action is 'drink' (the root). 'Cats' depends on 'drink' as the subject, and 'milk' depends on 'drink' as the object. The parser draws arrows showing: \n• drink ➔ Cats (subject)\n• drink ➔ milk (object)

cards.title: The Connection Myth
cards.body: People often think dependency parsing is the same as constituency parsing (phrase structure). Actually, constituency parsing groups words into nested chunks (like Noun Phrases), while dependency parsing connects individual words directly to each other.

cards.title: Parsing Comparison
cards.body: See the difference between grouping words into blocks versus linking words directly with arrows.

left.text: Groups words into nested boxes: [The [happy developer]] [wrote [code]].

right.text: Draws direct arrows: developer ➔ happy, wrote ➔ developer, wrote ➔ code.

exercises.prompt: In the sentence 'The loud dog barks', which relationship is correct?
exercises.explain: The verb 'barks' is the main action (head), and the adjective 'loud' describes the noun 'dog' (dependent). Therefore, 'loud' depends directly on 'dog'.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In a dependency parse tree, the main verb of the sentence is usually the 'root' (the starting point with no head of its own).
exercises.explain: True. In dependency grammar, there is typically one main word (usually the main verb) that has no parent. This word is called the root.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the relationship rule in dependency parsing.
exercises.sentence: A dependency link always connects a dominant _______ word to its helper dependent word.
exercises.explain: In dependency parsing, every link goes from a governor or 'head' word to a modifier or 'dependent' word.

exercises.prompt: Which of these demonstrates an AI dependency parser analyzing the nonsensical but grammatical phrase 'Green sleep furiously'?

options.text: An AI-generated dependency parse showing 'sleep' as the head, with 'Green' as its subject.
A simple list of words sorted alphabetically.
A dictionary definition of the word 'furiously'.

exercises.explain: AI parsers look at grammatical relationships (like subject-verb). 'Green sleep furiously' has no logical meaning, but it still has grammatical structure that a dependency parser can map. Option A represents this mapping.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following best describes how dependency parsing analyzes a sentence?

options.text: It groups sentences into nested phrases like Noun Phrases and Verb Phrases.
It links words directly to each other using one-to-one grammatical relationships.
It only counts the number of nouns and verbs in a sentence without linking them.
It randomly pairs words together regardless of their grammatical function.

exercises.explain: Dependency parsing reveals the grammatical backbone of a sentence by drawing direct, directed arrows from head words (like verbs) to their dependent words (like subjects and objects). It focuses on binary grammatical links rather than nested groupings.
